    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 224
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/224](https://www.erdosproblems.com/224)
    23→
    24→If $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ is any set of $2^d + 1$ points then some three
    25→points in $A$ determine an obtuse angle.
    26→
    27→## Known results
    28→
    29→- For $d = 2$ this is trivial.
    30→- For $d = 3$ there is an unpublished proof by Kuiper and Boerdijk.
    31→- The general case was proved by Danzer and Grünbaum [DaGr62].
    32→
    33→[DaGr62] Danzer, L. and Grünbaum, B., _Über zwei Probleme bezüglich konvexer
    34→Körper von P. Erdős und von V.L. Klee_, Math. Z. 79 (1962), 95–99.
    35→-/
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos224
    38→
    39→/--
    40→Erdős Problem 224 (Danzer–Grünbaum [DaGr62]):
    41→Any set of at least $2^d + 1$ points in $\mathbb{R}^d$ contains three distinct
    42→points $a$, $b$, $c$ such that the angle at $b$ is obtuse
    43→(i.e., $\langle a - b, c - b \rangle < 0$).
    44→-/
    45→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    46→theorem erdos_224 (d : ℕ) (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d)))
    47→    (hA : 2 ^ d + 1 ≤ A.card) :
    48→    ∃ a ∈ A, ∃ b ∈ A, ∃ c ∈ A,
    49→      a ≠ b ∧ b ≠ c ∧ a ≠ c ∧
    50→      @inner ℝ _ _ (a - b) (c - b) < 0 := by
    51→  sorry
    52→
    53→end Erdos224
    54→

    22→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Logic.Equiv.Fin.Rotate

    24→
    25→scoped[EuclideanGeometry] notation "ℝ²" => EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)
    26→
    27→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    28→
    29→/-- Oriented angles make sense in 2d.
    30→
    31→Note: this can't blindly be added to mathlib as it creates an "instance diamond"
    32→with an instance for modules satisfying `is_empty`. -/
    33→noncomputable instance Module.orientedEuclideanSpaceFinTwo : Module.Oriented ℝ ℝ² (Fin 2) :=
    34→  ⟨Basis.orientation <| PiLp.basisFun 2 _ _⟩
    35→
    36→/-- Two dimensional euclidean space is two-dimensional. -/
    37→instance fact_finrank_euclideanSpace_fin_two : Fact (Module.finrank ℝ ℝ² = 2) :=
    38→  ⟨finrank_euclideanSpace_fin⟩
    39→
    40→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    41→
    42→open scoped Real
    43→
    44→namespace EuclideanGeometry
    45→
    46→variable {V P : Type*} {n : ℕ}
    47→
    48→variable [NormedAddCommGroup V] [InnerProductSpace ℝ V] [MetricSpace P] [NormedAddTorsor V P]
    49→
    50→variable [Module.Oriented ℝ V (Fin 2)] [Fact (Module.finrank ℝ V = 2)] {p : Fin n → P}
    51→
    52→/-- We say a subset `A` of points in the plane is non-trilinear
    53→if it contains no three points that lie on the same line. -/
    54→def NonTrilinear (A : Set P) : Prop :=
    55→  A.Triplewise (fun x y z ↦ ¬ Collinear ℝ {x, y, z})
    56→
    57→/-- `ConvexIndep S` means that `S` consists of extremal points of its convex hull,
    58→i.e., the point set encloses a convex shape.
    59→Also known as a "convex-independent set". -/
    60→def ConvexIndep (S : Set ℝ²) : Prop :=
    61→  ∀ a ∈ S, a ∉ convexHull ℝ (S \ {a})
    62→
    63→/-- The set `P` contains a convex `n`-gon.
    64→See also `IsConvexPolygon`. -/
    65→def HasConvexNGon (n : ℕ) (P : Set ℝ²) : Prop :=
    66→  ∃ S : Finset ℝ², S.card = n ∧ ↑S ⊆ P ∧ ConvexIndep S
    67→
    68→/-- The statement that a sequence of points form a counter-clockwise convex polygon. -/
    69→def IsCcwConvexPolygon (p : Fin n → P) : Prop :=
    70→  ∀ ⦃i j k⦄, i < j → j < k → (∡ (p i) (p j) (p k)).sign = 1
    71→
    72→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    73→  (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p i) (p j) (p k)).sign = 1 := hp hij hjk
    74→
    75→set_option linter.docPrime false in
    76→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle' (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    77→    (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p j) (p k) (p i)).sign = 1 := by
    78→  rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
    79→  exact hp hij hjk
    80→
    81→set_option linter.docPrime false in
    82→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle'' (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    83→    (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p k) (p i) (p j)).sign = 1 := by
    84→  rw [← EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
    85→  exact hp hij hjk
    86→
    87→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle_finRotate (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p)
    88→    (hn : 3 ≤ n) (i : Fin n) :
    89→    (∡ (p i) (p <| finRotate _ i) (p <| finRotate _ (finRotate _ i))).sign = 1 := by
    90→  obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ := le_iff_exists_add'.mp hn
    91→  by_cases hi : i = Fin.last (n + 2)
    92→  · rw [hi, finRotate_last, finRotate_apply_zero]
    93→    exact hp.sign_oangle'' Fin.zero_lt_one Fin.one_lt_last
    94→  by_cases hi' : finRotate _ i = Fin.last (n + 2)
    95→  · rw [hi', finRotate_last]
    96→    refine hp.sign_oangle' ?_ ((Fin.le_last _).lt_of_ne hi)
    97→    rw [Fin.pos_iff_ne_zero]
    98→    rintro rfl
    99→    rw [finRotate_apply_zero] at hi'
   100→    exact Fin.one_lt_last.ne hi'
   101→  apply hp.sign_oangle <;> apply lt_finRotate_of_ne_last <;> assumption
   102→
   103→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_zero (p : Fin 0 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := finZeroElim
   104→
   105→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_one (p : Fin 1 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := by intro; omega
   106→
   107→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_two (p : Fin 2 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := by intro; omega
   108→
   109→set_option linter.docPrime false in
   110→theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_four' {p : Fin 4 → P} :
   111→    IsCcwConvexPolygon p ↔ (∡ (p 0) (p 1) (p 2)).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ (p 1) (p 2) (p 3)).sign = 1 ∧
   112→    (∡ (p 2) (p 3) (p 0)).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ (p 3) (p 0) (p 1)).sign = 1 := by
   113→  refine ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, fun ⟨h1, h2, h3, h4⟩ ↦ ?_⟩
   114→  · obtain ⟨h01, h12, h23⟩ : (0 : Fin 4) < 1 ∧ (1 : Fin 4) < 2 ∧ (2 : Fin 4) < 3 := by simp
   115→    · repeat' constructor
   116→      · exact h.sign_oangle h01 h12
   117→      · exact h.sign_oangle h12 h23
   118→      · exact h.sign_oangle' (h01.trans h12) h23
   119→      · exact h.sign_oangle'' h01 (h12.trans h23)
   120→  · intro i j k hij hjk
   121→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   122→    · exact h1
   123→    · rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
   124→      exact h4
   125→    · rw [← EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
   126→      exact h3
   127→    · exact h2
   128→
   129→@[simp]
   130→theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_four (A B C D : P) :
   131→    IsCcwConvexPolygon ![A, B, C, D] ↔
   132→      (∡ A B C).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ B C D).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ C D A).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ D A B).sign = 1 :=
   133→  isCcwConvexPolygon_four'
   134→
   135→/-- The statement that a sequence of points form a convex polygon. -/
   136→def IsConvexPolygon {n : ℕ} (p : Fin n → P) : Prop :=
   137→  IsCcwConvexPolygon p ∨ IsCcwConvexPolygon fun i => p (-i)
   138→
   139→/-- Three affine independent points always form a convex polygon. -/
   140→theorem isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependent {A B C : P}
   141→    (hABC : AffineIndependent ℝ ![A, B, C]) : IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] := by
   142→  rw [← oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ← Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff] at hABC
   143→  cases hsABC : (∡ A B C).sign
   144→  · exact (hABC hsABC).elim
   145→  · right
   146→    intro i j k hij hjk
   147→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   148→    rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rev, Real.Angle.sign_neg, neg_eq_iff_eq_neg]
   149→    exact (oangle_rotate_sign A B C).trans hsABC
   150→  · left
   151→    intro i j k hij hjk
   152→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   153→    exact hsABC
   154→
   155→theorem isConvexPolygon_three_iff_affineIndependent {A B C : P} :
   156→    IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] ↔ AffineIndependent ℝ ![A, B, C] := by
   157→  refine ⟨fun h => ?_, isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependent⟩
   158→  rw [← oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ← Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff]
   159→  let p := ![A, B, C]
   160→  change IsConvexPolygon p at h
   161→  change Real.Angle.sign (∡ (p 0) (p 1) (p 2)) ≠ 0
   162→  cases h with
   163→  | inl h =>
   164→    rw [h.sign_oangle (by simp) (by simp)]
   165→    rintro ⟨⟩
   166→  | inr h =>
   167→    suffices Real.Angle.sign (∡ (p 0) (p 2) (p 1)) = 1 by rw [← oangle_swap₂₃_sign, this]; rintro ⟨⟩
   168→    exact h.sign_oangle (i := 0) (j := 1) (k := 2) (by simp) (by simp)
   169→
   170→theorem isConvexPolygon_triangle (t : Affine.Triangle ℝ P) : IsConvexPolygon t.points := by
   171→  have : t.points = ![t.points 0, t.points 1, t.points 2] := by ext i; fin_cases i <;> rfl
   172→  rw [this, isConvexPolygon_three_iff_affineIndependent, ← this]
   173→  exact t.independent
   174→
   175→noncomputable def triangle_area (a b c : P) : ℝ :=
   176→  positiveOrientation.areaForm (a -ᵥ c) (b -ᵥ c) / 2
   177→
   178→lemma triangle_area_eq_det (a b c : ℝ²) :
   179→    triangle_area a b c =
   180→    Matrix.det !![a 0, b 0, c 0;
   181→                  a 1, b 1, c 1;
   182→                  1,   1,   1] / 2 := by
   183→  rw [triangle_area, Orientation.areaForm_to_volumeForm,
   184→    positiveOrientation.volumeForm_robust (EuclideanSpace.basisFun (Fin 2) ℝ) rfl, Module.Basis.det_apply]
   185→  suffices (a 0 - c 0) * (b 1 - c 1) - (b 0 - c 0) * (a 1 - c 1) =
   186→      a 0 * b 1 - a 0 * c 1 - b 0 * a 1 + b 0 * c 1 + c 0 * a 1 - c 0 * b 1 by
   187→    simp [Matrix.det_fin_two, Matrix.det_fin_three, Module.Basis.toMatrix, this]
   188→  ring
   189→
   190→/--
   191→Given a finite set of points in the plane, we define the number of distinct distances between pairs
   192→of points.
   193→-/
   194→noncomputable def distinctDistances (points : Finset ℝ²) : ℕ :=
   195→  (points.offDiag.image fun (pair : ℝ² × ℝ²) => dist pair.1 pair.2).card
   196→
   197→end EuclideanGeometry
   198→
   199→
   200→def IsIsosceles {α : Type*} [Dist α] (p q r : α) : Prop :=
   201→  dist p q = dist q r ∨ dist q r = dist r p ∨ dist r p = dist p q
   202→
   203→nonrec def Set.IsIsosceles {α : Type} [Dist α] (A : Set α) :=
   204→  Nonempty A ∧ A.Triplewise (IsIsosceles · · ·)
   205→

1→/-

    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 504
    21→
    22→Determine the maximum guaranteed angle α_N for N-point planar sets: the supremum of all
    23→angles α such that every set of N points in the plane contains three distinct points forming
    24→an angle of at least α.
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/504](https://www.erdosproblems.com/504)
    27→-/
    28→
    29→namespace Erdos504
    30→
    31→/--
    32→The angle at point $y$ determined by three points $x$, $y$, $z$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$:
    33→the angle between vectors $(x - y)$ and $(z - y)$, computed as
    34→$\arccos$ of their normalized inner product. Returns a value in $[0, \pi]$.
    35→-/
    36→noncomputable def angleAt (x y z : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) : ℝ :=
    37→  let u := x - y
    38→  let v := z - y
    39→  Real.arccos (@inner ℝ _ _ u v / (‖u‖ * ‖v‖))
    40→
    41→/--
    42→$\alpha_N$: the maximum guaranteed angle for $N$-point planar sets.
    43→The supremum of all $\alpha \in [0, \pi]$ such that every set of $N$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$
    44→contains three distinct points $x$, $y$, $z$ with angle at $y$ at least $\alpha$.
    45→-/
    46→noncomputable def maxGuaranteedAngle (N : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    47→  sSup {α : ℝ | 0 ≤ α ∧ α ≤ Real.pi ∧
    48→    ∀ (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    49→      A.card = N →
    50→      ∃ x ∈ A, ∃ y ∈ A, ∃ z ∈ A,
    51→        x ≠ y ∧ y ≠ z ∧ x ≠ z ∧
    52→        angleAt x y z ≥ α}
    53→
    54→/--
    55→Erdős Problem 504 (Blumenthal's problem, solved by Sendov):
    56→
    57→Let $\alpha_N$ be the supremum of all $0 \leq \alpha \leq \pi$ such that in every set
    58→$A \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ of size $N$ there exist three distinct points $x, y, z \in A$
    59→such that the angle at $y$ (between rays $yx$ and $yz$) is at least $\alpha$.
    60→Determine $\alpha_N$.
    61→
    62→Sendov (1993) proved that for $n \geq 3$ and $2^{n-1} < N \leq 2^n$:
    63→$$\alpha_N = \pi(1 - 1/n) \quad \text{when } N > 2^{n-1} + 2^{n-3}$$
    64→$$\alpha_N = \pi(1 - 1/(2n-1)) \quad \text{when } N \leq 2^{n-1} + 2^{n-3}$$
    65→-/
    66→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    67→theorem erdos_504 :
    68→    ∀ (N : ℕ), 4 < N →
    69→    ∀ (n : ℕ), 3 ≤ n → 2 ^ (n - 1) < N → N ≤ 2 ^ n →
    70→      maxGuaranteedAngle N =
    71→        answer(if 2 ^ (n - 1) + 2 ^ (n - 3) < N
    72→          then Real.pi * (1 - 1 / (n : ℝ))
    73→          else Real.pi * (1 - 1 / (2 * (n : ℝ) - 1))) := by
    74→  sorry
    75→
    76→end Erdos504
    77→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 988
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/988](https://www.erdosproblems.com/988)
    23→
    24→If $P \subseteq S^2$ is a subset of the unit sphere then define the discrepancy
    25→$$D(P) = \max_C \left| |C \cap P| - \alpha_C |P| \right|,$$
    26→where the maximum is taken over all spherical caps $C$, and $\alpha_C$ is the
    27→appropriately normalised measure of $C$.
    28→
    29→Is it true that $\min_{|P|=n} D(P) \to \infty$ as $n \to \infty$?
    30→
    31→This was proved (in any number of dimensions) by Schmidt [Sc69b].
    32→
    33→[Er64b] Erdős, P., *On some problems in number theory*, 1964.
    34→
    35→[Sc69b] Schmidt, W. M., *Irregularities of distribution. IV.* Invent. Math. 7
    36→(1969), 55–82.
    37→-/
    38→
    39→open Classical
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos988
    42→
    43→/--
    44→Erdős Problem 988 [Er64b]:
    45→
    46→For every $M > 0$, there exists $N_0$ such that for every finite set $P$ of points
    47→on the unit sphere $S^2$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with $|P| \geq N_0$, there exists a spherical cap
    48→$C(v,t) = \{x \in S^2 : \langle x, v \rangle \geq t\}$ (with $v$ a unit vector and
    49→$t \in [-1,1]$) such that the discrepancy
    50→$\left||C(v,t) \cap P| - \frac{1-t}{2} \cdot |P|\right| \geq M$.
    51→
    52→Here $\frac{1-t}{2}$ is the normalised surface area measure of the cap $C(v,t)$ on $S^2$.
    53→-/
    54→@[category research solved, AMS 11 52]
    55→theorem erdos_988 :
    56→    answer(True) ↔
    57→    ∀ M : ℝ, M > 0 →
    58→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3))),
    59→      (∀ p ∈ P, ‖p‖ = 1) →
    60→      P.card ≥ N₀ →
    61→      ∃ (v : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3)) (t : ℝ),
    62→        ‖v‖ = 1 ∧ -1 ≤ t ∧ t ≤ 1 ∧
    63→        |((P.filter (fun x => @inner ℝ _ _ x v ≥ t)).card : ℝ) -
    64→          (1 - t) / 2 * (P.card : ℝ)| ≥ M := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→end Erdos988
    68→

    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 991
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/991](https://www.erdosproblems.com/991)
    23→
    24→Suppose $A = \{w_1, \ldots, w_n\} \subset S^2$ maximises $\prod_{i<j} |w_i - w_j|$ over all
    25→possible sets of size $n$ (such sets are called Fekete points).
    26→
    27→Is it true that
    28→$$
    29→  \max_C \left| |A \cap C| - \alpha_C \cdot n \right| = o(n),
    30→$$
    31→where the maximum is taken over all spherical caps $C$ and $\alpha_C$ is the area
    32→of $C$ (normalised so that the entire sphere has area $1$)?
    33→
    34→This was proved. Brauchart [Br08] showed $O(n^{3/4})$ and Marzo–Mas [MaMa21]
    35→improved this to $O(n^{2/3})$.
    36→
    37→[Br08] Brauchart, J. S., *Optimal discrete Riesz energy and discrepancy*,
    38→Uniform Distribution Theory 3 (2008), no. 1, 47–78.
    39→
    40→[MaMa21] Marzo, J. and Mas, A., *Discrepancy of minimal Riesz energy points*,
    41→J. Approx. Theory 264 (2021), 105538.
    42→-/
    43→
    44→open Classical Finset BigOperators
    45→
    46→namespace Erdos991
    47→
    48→/-- The product of pairwise distances of a finite set of points. -/
    49→noncomputable def pairwiseDistProd (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3))) : ℝ :=
    50→  ∏ p ∈ P.offDiag, ‖p.1 - p.2‖
    51→
    52→/-- A finite set of points on $S^2$ maximises the pairwise distance product
    53→among all sets of the same cardinality on $S^2$. -/
    54→def IsMaxPairwiseDist (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3))) : Prop :=
    55→  (∀ p ∈ P, ‖p‖ = 1) ∧
    56→  ∀ (Q : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3))),
    57→    (∀ q ∈ Q, ‖q‖ = 1) → Q.card = P.card →
    58→    pairwiseDistProd Q ≤ pairwiseDistProd P
    59→
    60→/--
    61→Erdős Problem 991 [Er64b]:
    62→
    63→If $A = \{w_1, \ldots, w_n\} \subset S^2$ are Fekete points (maximising the product of
    64→pairwise distances), then the spherical cap discrepancy of $A$ is $o(n)$:
    65→for every $\varepsilon > 0$, if $n$ is large enough and $P$ is any set of $n$ Fekete points,
    66→then for every spherical cap $C(v,t) = \{x \in S^2 : \langle x,v \rangle \geq t\}$,
    67→$$
    68→  \left| |P \cap C(v,t)| - \frac{1-t}{2} \cdot n \right| \leq \varepsilon \cdot n.
    69→$$
    70→
    71→Here $\frac{1-t}{2}$ is the normalised surface area of the cap $C(v,t)$ on $S^2$.
    72→-/
    73→@[category research solved, AMS 31 52]
    74→theorem erdos_991 :
    75→    answer(True) ↔
    76→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    77→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3))),
    78→      IsMaxPairwiseDist P →
    79→      P.card ≥ N₀ →
    80→      ∀ (v : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3)) (t : ℝ),
    81→        ‖v‖ = 1 → -1 ≤ t → t ≤ 1 →
    82→        |((P.filter (fun x => @inner ℝ _ _ x v ≥ t)).card : ℝ) -
    83→          (1 - t) / 2 * (P.card : ℝ)| ≤ ε * (P.card : ℝ) := by
    84→  sorry
    85→
    86→end Erdos991
    87→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1121
    21→
    22→If circles in the plane with radii $r_1, \ldots, r_n$ cannot be separated into two non-empty
    23→groups by a line disjoint from all of them, then they can be covered by a single circle of radius
    24→$r = \sum r_i$.
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1121](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1121)
    27→
    28→[GoGo45] Goodman, A. W. and Goodman, R. E., *A circle covering theorem*,
    29→American Mathematical Monthly 52 (1945), 494-498.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open scoped BigOperators
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos1121
    35→
    36→/--
    37→Erdős Problem #1121 (proved by Goodman and Goodman [GoGo45]):
    38→
    39→If $C_1, \ldots, C_n$ are circles in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with radii $r_1, \ldots, r_n$ such that no
    40→line disjoint from all the circles divides them into two non-empty sets, then the circles can be
    41→covered by a circle of radius $r = \sum r_i$.
    42→
    43→A line in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is parameterized by a unit normal vector $v$ and offset $d$, defining
    44→$\ell = \{x : \langle x, v \rangle = d\}$. The closed disk $\bar{B}(c_i, r_i)$ is disjoint from
    45→$\ell$ when $|\langle c_i, v \rangle - d| > r_i$. The non-separability condition says that whenever
    46→all disks are disjoint from a line, they all lie on the same side.
    47→-/
    48→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    49→theorem erdos_1121
    50→    (n : ℕ)
    51→    (center : Fin n → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))
    52→    (radius : Fin n → ℝ)
    53→    (hr : ∀ i, 0 < radius i)
    54→    (hns : ∀ (v : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) (d : ℝ),
    55→      ‖v‖ = 1 →
    56→      (∀ i, |@inner ℝ _ _ (center i) v - d| > radius i) →
    57→      (∀ i j, @inner ℝ _ _ (center i) v > d ↔ @inner ℝ _ _ (center j) v > d)) :
    58→    ∃ p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2),
    59→      ∀ i, dist (center i) p + radius i ≤ ∑ j : Fin n, radius j := by
    60→  sorry
    61→
    62→end Erdos1121
    63→

    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 93
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/93](https://www.erdosproblems.com/93)
    23→
    24→If $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are in convex position (vertices of a convex polygon), then they
    25→determine at least $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ distinct distances. Proved by Altman.
    26→
    27→[Al63] Altman, E., _On a problem of P. Erdős_. Amer. Math. Monthly 70 (1963), 148-157.
    28→-/
    29→
    30→open scoped Classical
    31→
    32→namespace Erdos93
    33→
    34→/--
    35→A finite set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is in convex position if no point lies in the
    36→convex hull of the remaining points. Equivalently, the points are the vertices
    37→of a convex polygon.
    38→-/
    39→def InConvexPosition (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    40→  ∀ p ∈ A, p ∉ convexHull ℝ ((↑A : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) \ {p})
    41→
    42→/--
    43→The set of distinct pairwise distances between distinct points in a finite
    44→point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
    45→-/
    46→noncomputable def distinctDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Finset ℝ :=
    47→  A.offDiag.image (fun pq => dist pq.1 pq.2)
    48→
    49→/--
    50→Erdős Problem 93: If $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a convex polygon, then they
    51→determine at least $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ distinct distances. Proved by Altman [Al63].
    52→-/
    53→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    54→theorem erdos_93
    55→    (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)))
    56→    (hconv : InConvexPosition A)
    57→    (hn : 2 ≤ A.card) :
    58→    A.card / 2 ≤ (distinctDistances A).card := by
    59→  sorry
    60→
    61→end Erdos93
    62→

    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 94
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/94](https://www.erdosproblems.com/94)
    23→
    24→For $n$ points in convex position with distance multiplicities $f(u_i)$, the sum
    25→$\sum_i f(u_i)^2$ is $O(n^3)$. Proved by Lefmann and Thiele.
    26→
    27→[LeTh95] Lefmann, H. and Thiele, T., _Point sets with distinct distances_.
    28→Combinatorica 15 (1995), 379–408.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos94
    32→
    33→/--
    34→A finite set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is in convex position if no point lies in
    35→the convex hull of the remaining points. Equivalently, all points are
    36→vertices of their convex hull (they form a convex polygon).
    37→-/
    38→def ConvexPosition (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    39→  ∀ p ∈ A, p ∉ convexHull ℝ ((A : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) \ {p})
    40→
    41→/--
    42→The count of ordered quadruples $(a, b, c, d)$ from $A$ with $a \neq b$, $c \neq d$,
    43→and $\operatorname{dist}(a, b) = \operatorname{dist}(c, d)$. This equals
    44→$4 \cdot \sum_i f(u_i)^2$ where $f(u_i)$ counts unordered pairs at distance $u_i$.
    45→-/
    46→noncomputable def distSqSum (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    47→  let E := EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)
    48→  Set.ncard {x : E × E × E × E |
    49→    x.1 ∈ (A : Set E) ∧ x.2.1 ∈ (A : Set E) ∧
    50→    x.2.2.1 ∈ (A : Set E) ∧ x.2.2.2 ∈ (A : Set E) ∧
    51→    x.1 ≠ x.2.1 ∧ x.2.2.1 ≠ x.2.2.2 ∧
    52→    dist x.1 x.2.1 = dist x.2.2.1 x.2.2.2}
    53→
    54→/--
    55→Suppose $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are in convex position (they form the vertices of
    56→a convex polygon). Let $\{u_1, \ldots, u_t\}$ be the set of distinct pairwise
    57→distances, and let $f(u_i)$ denote the number of pairs at distance $u_i$. Then
    58→$$\sum_i f(u_i)^2 \ll n^3.$$
    59→
    60→This was proved by Lefmann and Thiele [LeTh95] under the weaker
    61→assumption that no three points are collinear.
    62→-/
    63→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    64→theorem erdos_94 :
    65→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    66→    ∀ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    67→      ConvexPosition A →
    68→      (distSqSum A : ℝ) ≤ C * (A.card : ℝ) ^ 3 := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→end Erdos94

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 96
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/96](https://www.erdosproblems.com/96)
    23→
    24→Erdős–Moser conjecture: if $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are in convex position, then the number
    25→of pairs at unit distance is $O(n)$.
    26→-/
    27→
    28→namespace Erdos96
    29→
    30→/--
    31→A finite set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is in convex position if no point lies in the
    32→convex hull of the remaining points. Equivalently, the points are the vertices
    33→of a convex polygon.
    34→-/
    35→def ConvexPosition (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    36→  ∀ p ∈ P, p ∉ convexHull ℝ (↑(P.erase p) : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)))
    37→
    38→/--
    39→The number of ordered pairs of distinct points in $P$ that are at unit distance.
    40→(Using ordered pairs; the number of unordered pairs is exactly half this,
    41→so the $O(n)$ bound is equivalent.)
    42→-/
    43→noncomputable def unitDistancePairCount (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    44→  (P.offDiag.filter (fun pq => dist pq.1 pq.2 = 1)).card
    45→
    46→/--
    47→Erdős Problem 96 (Erdős–Moser conjecture):
    48→If $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a convex polygon (are in convex position), then there
    49→are $O(n)$ many pairs which are distance $1$ apart.
    50→
    51→Formally: there exists an absolute constant $C > 0$ such that for every finite
    52→set $P$ of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ in convex position, the number of (ordered) pairs at
    53→unit distance is at most $C \cdot |P|$.
    54→-/
    55→@[category research open, AMS 52]
    56→theorem erdos_96 :
    57→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    58→    ∀ (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    59→      ConvexPosition P →
    60→      (unitDistancePairCount P : ℝ) ≤ C * (P.card : ℝ) := by
    61→  sorry
    62→
    63→end Erdos96

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 827
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/827](https://www.erdosproblems.com/827)
    23→
    24→Let $n_k$ be minimal such that if $n_k$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are in general
    25→position then there exists a subset of $k$ points such that all $\binom{k}{3}$
    26→triples determine circles of different radii.
    27→
    28→Erdős [Er75h] asked whether $n_k$ exists. In [Er78c] he gave a simple argument
    29→proving existence, but the argument was incorrect as noted by Martinez and
    30→Roldán-Pensado [MaRo15]. A corrected argument gives $n_k \ll k^9$.
    31→-/
    32→
    33→open Finset
    34→
    35→namespace Erdos827
    36→
    37→/-- Points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are in general position if no three are collinear. -/
    38→def InGeneralPosition (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    39→  ∀ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    40→    S ⊆ P → S.card = 3 → ¬Collinear ℝ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)))
    41→
    42→/-- The circumradius of three points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, defined via Heron's formula.
    43→    For a non-degenerate triangle with side lengths $a$, $b$, $c$ and semiperimeter
    44→    $s = (a+b+c)/2$, the circumradius is $R = \frac{abc}{4\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}}$.
    45→    Returns $0$ for degenerate (collinear) configurations. -/
    46→noncomputable def circumradius (p₁ p₂ p₃ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) : ℝ :=
    47→  let a := dist p₁ p₂
    48→  let b := dist p₂ p₃
    49→  let c := dist p₁ p₃
    50→  let s := (a + b + c) / 2
    51→  let area_sq := s * (s - a) * (s - b) * (s - c)
    52→  if area_sq > 0 then
    53→    (a * b * c) / (4 * Real.sqrt area_sq)
    54→  else 0
    55→
    56→/-- All $\binom{k}{3}$ triples of distinct points in $S$ determine circles of pairwise
    57→    distinct radii. -/
    58→def AllDistinctCircumradii (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    59→  ∀ a ∈ S, ∀ b ∈ S, ∀ c ∈ S,
    60→  ∀ d ∈ S, ∀ e ∈ S, ∀ f ∈ S,
    61→    a ≠ b → a ≠ c → b ≠ c →
    62→    d ≠ e → d ≠ f → e ≠ f →
    63→    ({a, b, c} : Finset _) ≠ ({d, e, f} : Finset _) →
    64→    circumradius a b c ≠ circumradius d e f
    65→
    66→/--
    67→**Erdős Problem 827** [Er75h, Er78c, Er92e]:
    68→
    69→For every $k \geq 3$, there exists $n$ such that any set of $n$ points in
    70→$\mathbb{R}^2$ in general position contains a $k$-element subset whose
    71→$\binom{k}{3}$ triples all determine circumscribed circles of pairwise
    72→different radii.
    73→
    74→Martinez and Roldán-Pensado [MaRo15] proved this with $n \leq C \cdot k^9$.
    75→-/
    76→@[category research solved, AMS 5 51]
    77→theorem erdos_827 (k : ℕ) (hk : k ≥ 3) :
    78→    ∃ n : ℕ, ∀ P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    79→      P.card ≥ n →
    80→      InGeneralPosition P →
    81→      ∃ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    82→        S ⊆ P ∧ S.card = k ∧ AllDistinctCircumradii S := by
    83→  sorry
    84→
    85→end Erdos827
    86→

    21→
    22→Is it true that, for every $k$, there is some $f(k)$ such that if $G$ has chromatic number
    23→$\geq f(k)$ then $G$ contains a triangle-free subgraph with chromatic number $\geq k$?
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/923](https://www.erdosproblems.com/923)
    26→

    29→-/
    30→

    36→Is it true that, for every $k$, there is some $f(k)$ such that if $G$ has chromatic number
    37→$\geq f(k)$ then $G$ contains a triangle-free subgraph with chromatic number $\geq k$?
    38→
    39→Proved by Rödl [Ro77]. This is the $r = 4$ special case of problem \#108.
    40→Triangle-free is formalized here as girth $\geq 4$ (no cycles of length 3).
    41→-/
    42→@[category research solved, AMS 5]

    49→            ∃ H : G.Subgraph,
    50→              (k : ℕ∞) ≤ H.coe.chromaticNumber ∧
    51→              (4 : ℕ∞) ≤ H.coe.egirth := by
    52→  sorry
    53→
    54→end Erdos923

    22→Is it true that the maximum number of k-rich lines determined by n points in the plane with no

    28→namespace Erdos588

    30→/--
    31→A finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has no $(k+1)$ collinear points if every $(k+1)$-element

    33→-/
    34→def NoKPlusOneCollinear (k : ℕ) (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    35→  ∀ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    36→    S ⊆ P → S.card = k + 1 → ¬Collinear ℝ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)))
    37→
    38→/--
    39→The number of $k$-rich lines: the number of distinct affine lines in $\mathbb{R}^2$ that
    40→contain at least $k$ points from $P$.
    41→-/
    42→noncomputable def kRichLineCount (k : ℕ) (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    43→  Set.ncard {L : AffineSubspace ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) |
    44→    Module.finrank ℝ L.direction = 1 ∧
    45→    k ≤ Set.ncard {p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) | p ∈ (P : Set _) ∧ p ∈ L}}
    46→
    47→/--
    48→Let $f_k(n)$ be the maximum number of lines containing at least $k$ points,
    49→over all configurations of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with no $k+1$ collinear.
    50→Is it true that $f_k(n) = o(n^2)$ for all $k \geq 4$?
    51→
    52→This is a generalisation of Problem \#101 (the case $k = 4$). The restriction
    53→to $k \geq 4$ is necessary since Sylvester showed $f_3(n) = n^2/6 + O(n)$.
    54→
    55→Formally: for every $k \geq 4$ and every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that for
    56→all $n \geq N$ and every set $P$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with no $k+1$ collinear, the
    57→count of $k$-rich lines is at most $\varepsilon \cdot n^2$.
    58→-/
    59→@[category research open, AMS 5 52]
    60→theorem erdos_588 : answer(sorry) ↔
    61→    ∀ k : ℕ, k ≥ 4 →
    62→      ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    63→        ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N →
    64→          ∀ P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    65→            P.card = n →
    66→            NoKPlusOneCollinear k P →
    67→            (kRichLineCount k P : ℝ) ≤ ε * (n : ℝ) ^ 2 := by
    68→  sorry
    69→
    70→end Erdos588
    71→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 210
    21→
    22→Let $f(n)$ denote the minimum number of ordinary lines determined by $n$
    23→non-collinear points in the plane. Erdős asked whether $f(n) \to \infty$ and
    24→how fast it grows.
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/210](https://www.erdosproblems.com/210)
    27→-/
    28→
    29→open Classical
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos210
    32→
    33→/-- Points in the Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    34→abbrev Point2 := EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)
    35→
    36→/-- Point $r$ lies on the line through distinct points $p$ and $q$. -/
    37→def LiesOnLine (p q r : Point2) : Prop :=
    38→  ∃ t : ℝ, r - p = t • (q - p)
    39→
    40→/-- A finite set of points is not all collinear: there exist three
    41→non-collinear points in $S$. -/
    42→def NotAllCollinear (S : Finset Point2) : Prop :=
    43→  ∃ p ∈ S, ∃ q ∈ S, ∃ r ∈ S, p ≠ q ∧ p ≠ r ∧ q ≠ r ∧ ¬LiesOnLine p q r
    44→
    45→/-- An ordered pair $(p, q)$ determines an ordinary line for $S$ if $p \neq q$,
    46→both lie in $S$, and no third point of $S$ lies on the line through $p$ and $q$. -/
    47→def IsOrdinaryPair (S : Finset Point2) (p q : Point2) : Prop :=
    48→  p ∈ S ∧ q ∈ S ∧ p ≠ q ∧ ∀ r ∈ S, r ≠ p → r ≠ q → ¬LiesOnLine p q r
    49→
    50→/-- The number of ordinary lines determined by $S$.
    51→Counts ordered pairs forming ordinary lines and divides by $2$. -/
    52→noncomputable def ordinaryLineCount (S : Finset Point2) : ℕ :=
    53→  ((S ×ˢ S).filter (fun pq => IsOrdinaryPair S pq.1 pq.2)).card / 2
    54→
    55→/--
    56→Let $f(n)$ be minimal such that for any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, not all on a line,
    57→there are at least $f(n)$ ordinary lines (lines containing exactly $2$ of the
    58→points). Green and Tao proved that $f(n) \geq n/2$ for all sufficiently large $n$,
    59→resolving the conjecture of Erdős and de Bruijn and strengthening earlier
    60→results of Motzkin ($f(n) \to \infty$), Kelly-Moser ($f(n) \geq 3n/7$), and
    61→Csima-Sawyer ($f(n) \geq 6n/13$).
    62→-/
    63→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    64→theorem erdos_210 :
    65→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ (S : Finset Point2),
    66→      S.card ≥ N₀ →
    67→      NotAllCollinear S →
    68→      ordinaryLineCount S ≥ S.card / 2 := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→end Erdos210
    72→

    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 217
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/217](https://www.erdosproblems.com/217)
    23→
    24→For which $n$ are there $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, no three on a line and no four on a
    25→circle, which determine $n-1$ distinct distances and so that (in some ordering
    26→of the distances) the $i$-th distance occurs $i$ times?
    27→
    28→An example with $n=4$ is an isosceles triangle with the point in the centre.
    29→Erdős originally believed this was impossible for $n \geq 5$, but Pomerance
    30→constructed a set with $n = 5$, and Palásti proved such sets exist for all $n \leq 8$.
    31→Erdős believed this is impossible for all sufficiently large $n$.
    32→
    33→[Er83c] [Er87b] [Er97e]
    34→-/
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos217
    37→
    38→/-- Three points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are collinear if the cross product of $(q - p)$ and $(r - p)$
    39→vanishes, i.e. the signed area of the triangle is zero. -/
    40→def Collinear3 (p q r : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) : Prop :=
    41→  (q 0 - p 0) * (r 1 - p 1) = (q 1 - p 1) * (r 0 - p 0)
    42→
    43→/-- A finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has no three collinear points. -/
    44→def NoThreeCollinear (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    45→  ∀ p ∈ S, ∀ q ∈ S, ∀ r ∈ S, p ≠ q → p ≠ r → q ≠ r → ¬Collinear3 p q r
    46→
    47→/-- Four points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are concyclic if they are all equidistant from some center. -/
    48→def Concyclic4 (p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) : Prop :=
    49→  ∃ (c : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) (r : ℝ),
    50→    dist p₁ c = r ∧ dist p₂ c = r ∧ dist p₃ c = r ∧ dist p₄ c = r
    51→
    52→/-- A finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has no four concyclic points. -/
    53→def NoFourConcyclic (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    54→  ∀ p₁ ∈ S, ∀ p₂ ∈ S, ∀ p₃ ∈ S, ∀ p₄ ∈ S,
    55→    p₁ ≠ p₂ → p₁ ≠ p₃ → p₁ ≠ p₄ → p₂ ≠ p₃ → p₂ ≠ p₄ → p₃ ≠ p₄ →
    56→    ¬Concyclic4 p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄
    57→
    58→/-- A set $S$ of $n$ points has the distance multiplicity property if there exist $n-1$
    59→distinct pairwise distances such that (in some ordering) the $j$-th distance
    60→occurs exactly $j$ times as an unordered pair. We count ordered pairs from
    61→`S.offDiag`, so the $j$-th distance yields $2(j+1)$ ordered pairs (since
    62→`Fin (n-1)` is 0-indexed). -/
    63→def HasDistanceMultiplicityProperty (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    64→  ∃ (d : Fin (S.card - 1) → ℝ),
    65→    Function.Injective d ∧
    66→    (∀ p ∈ S, ∀ q ∈ S, p ≠ q → ∃ i, dist p q = d i) ∧
    67→    (∀ i : Fin (S.card - 1),
    68→      (S.offDiag.filter (fun pq => dist pq.1 pq.2 = d i)).card = 2 * (i.val + 1))
    69→
    70→/--
    71→Erdős Conjecture (Problem 217) [Er83c, Er87b, Er97e]:
    72→
    73→For all sufficiently large $n$, there does not exist a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$,
    74→no three on a line and no four on a circle, which determine $n-1$ distinct
    75→distances such that (in some ordering) the $i$-th distance occurs exactly $i$ times.
    76→
    77→Known: such configurations exist for all $n \leq 8$ (Palásti). Erdős conjectured
    78→this is impossible for all sufficiently large $n$.
    79→-/
    80→@[category research open, AMS 5 52]
    81→theorem erdos_217 :
    82→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    83→      N₀ ≤ S.card →
    84→      NoThreeCollinear S →
    85→      NoFourConcyclic S →
    86→      ¬HasDistanceMultiplicityProperty S := by
    87→  sorry
    88→
    89→end Erdos217
    90→

    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 506
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/506](https://www.erdosproblems.com/506)
    23→
    24→What is the minimum number of circles determined by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$,
    25→not all on a circle and not all on a line?
    26→
    27→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_, 1961, p. 245.
    28→
    29→[El67] Elliott, P. D. T. A., _On the number of circles determined by $n$ points_, 1967.
    30→
    31→[PuSm] Purdy, G. and Smith, J. W., _Lines, circles, and the number of determined circles_.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open Finset
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos506
    37→
    38→/-- All points in $S$ lie on a common circle with positive radius. -/
    39→noncomputable def AllOnCircle (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    40→  ∃ c : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2), ∃ r : ℝ, 0 < r ∧ ∀ p ∈ S, dist p c = r
    41→
    42→/-- All points in $S$ are collinear: they lie on a common affine line. -/
    43→noncomputable def AllCollinear (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    44→  ∃ a b : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2), a ≠ b ∧
    45→    ∀ p ∈ S, ∃ t : ℝ, p = a + t • (b - a)
    46→
    47→/-- The number of distinct circles determined by $S$. A circle is identified by its
    48→center and positive radius. It is "determined" by $S$ if at least $3$ points of $S$
    49→lie on it. -/
    50→noncomputable def numDeterminedCircles (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    51→  Set.ncard {p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) × ℝ |
    52→    0 < p.2 ∧ 3 ≤ Set.ncard {q ∈ (↑S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) | dist q p.1 = p.2}}
    53→
    54→/-- The minimum number of circles determined by any configuration of $n$ points in
    55→$\mathbb{R}^2$ that are neither all on a circle nor all collinear. Returns $0$ when
    56→no such configuration exists. -/
    57→noncomputable def minDeterminedCircles (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    58→  sInf (numDeterminedCircles '' {S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) |
    59→    S.card = n ∧ ¬AllOnCircle S ∧ ¬AllCollinear S})
    60→
    61→/--
    62→Erdős Problem 506 [Er61, p. 245]:
    63→
    64→What is the minimum number of circles determined by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, not all on a
    65→circle (and not all on a line)?
    66→
    67→Elliott [El67] proved that for $n > 393$ points not all on a circle or a line, the
    68→points determine at least $\binom{n-1}{2}$ distinct circles. Purdy and Smith [PuSm]
    69→corrected this to the sharper bound $\binom{n-1}{2} + 1 - \lfloor(n-1)/2\rfloor$, which is best
    70→possible (witnessed by $n-1$ points on a circle and one point off it).
    71→-/
    72→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    73→theorem erdos_506 : ∀ n : ℕ, 393 < n →
    74→    minDeterminedCircles n =
    75→      answer(Nat.choose (n - 1) 2 + 1 - (n - 1) / 2) := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos506
    79→

    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 507
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→- [erdosproblems.com/507](https://www.erdosproblems.com/507)
    24→- [CPZ23] Cohen, Alex, Cosmin Pohoata, and Dmitrii Zakharov. "A new upper bound for the Heilbronn
    25→  triangle problem." arXiv preprint arXiv:2305.18253 (2023).
    26→- [CPZ24] Cohen, Alex, Cosmin Pohoata, and Dmitrii Zakharov. "Lower bounds for incidences."
    27→  Inventiones mathematicae (2025): 1-74.
    28→- [KPS82] Komlós, János, János Pintz, and Endre Szemerédi. "A lower bound for Heilbronn's problem."
    29→  Journal of the London Mathematical Society 2.1 (1982): 13-24.
    30→- [KPS81] Komlós, János, János Pintz, and Endre Szemerédi. "On Heilbronn's triangle problem."
    31→  Journal of the London Mathematical Society 2.3 (1981): 385-396.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open Asymptotics Filter Topology
    35→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos507
    38→
    39→/--
    40→The minimum area of a triangle determined by three distinct points in a set `S`.
    41→-/
    42→noncomputable def minTriangleArea (S : Finset ℝ²) : ℝ :=
    43→  sInf {EuclideanGeometry.triangle_area (t.points 0) (t.points 1) (t.points 2) |
    44→    (t : Affine.Triangle ℝ ℝ²) (_ : ∀ i, t.points i ∈ S)}
    45→
    46→/--
    47→$\alpha(n)$ is the supremum of `minTriangleArea S` over all sets `S` of $n$ points in the unit disk.
    48→-/
    49→noncomputable def α (n : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    50→  sSup (minTriangleArea '' { S : Finset ℝ² |
    51→    S.card = n ∧ ↑S ⊆ Metric.closedBall (0 : ℝ²) 1 ∧ ¬ Collinear ℝ (S : Set ℝ²) })
    52→
    53→/--
    54→Current best lower bound [KPS82].
    55→-/
    56→noncomputable def lowerBest (n : ℕ) : ℝ := Real.log n / (n : ℝ) ^ 2
    57→
    58→/--
    59→The "Barrier" function: n^(-7/6) used for the best upper bound [CPZ24].
    60→-/
    61→noncomputable def upperBarrier (n : ℕ) : ℝ := 1 / (n : ℝ) ^ ((7 : ℝ) / 6)
    62→
    63→/--
    64→Let $\alpha(n)$ be such that every set of $n$ points in the unit disk contains three points which
    65→determine a triangle of area at most $\alpha(n)$. Estimate $\alpha(n)$.
    66→-/
    67→@[category research open, AMS 51]
    68→theorem erdos_507.equivalent:
    69→    α ~[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
    70→  sorry
    71→
    72→/--
    73→Estimate a lower bound for$\alpha(n)$.
    74→-/
    75→@[category research open, AMS 51]
    76→theorem erdos_507.lower:
    77→    let ans := (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ)
    78→    (lowerBest =o[atTop] ans) ∧ (ans ≪ α) := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→/--
    82→Estimate an upper bound for$\alpha(n)$.
    83→-/
    84→@[category research open, AMS 51]
    85→theorem erdos_507.upper:
    86→    let ans := (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ)
    87→    (α ≪ ans) ∧ (ans =o[atTop] upperBarrier) := by
    88→  sorry
    89→
    90→/--
    91→It is trivial that $\alpha(n) \ll 1/n$.
    92→-/
    93→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
    94→theorem erdos_507.variants.upper_trivial : α ≪ (fun n ↦ 1 / (n : ℝ)) := by
    95→  sorry
    96→
    97→/--
    98→Erdős observed that $\alpha(n) \gg 1/n^2$.
    99→-/
   100→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   101→theorem erdos_507.variants.lower_erdos : α ≫ (fun n ↦ 1 / (n : ℝ) ^ 2) := by
   102→  sorry
   103→
   104→/--
   105→Current best lower bound [KPS82].
   106→-/
   107→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   108→theorem erdos_507.variants.lower_kps82 : lowerBest ≪ α := by
   109→  sorry
   110→
   111→/--
   112→Current best upper bound [CPZ24]: $\alpha(n) \ll n^{-7/6 + o(1)}$.
   113→-/
   114→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   115→theorem erdos_507.variants.upper_cpz24 :
   116→    ∃ (o : ℕ → ℝ), Tendsto o atTop (𝓝 0) ∧
   117→    α ≪ (fun n ↦ upperBarrier n * (n : ℝ) ^ o n) := by
   118→  sorry
   119→
   120→end Erdos507
   121→

     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→import Mathlib.Data.Finset.Sym
    17→import Mathlib.Topology.MetricSpace.Defs
    18→
    19→open scoped Finset
    20→
    21→variable {X : Type*} [MetricSpace X]
    22→
    23→/-- The number of pairs of points of a finite set `s` in a metric space that are distance 1 apart.
    24→-/
    25→noncomputable def unitDistNum (s : Finset X) : ℕ := #{p ∈ s.sym2 | dist p.out.1 p.out.2 = 1}
    26→

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→
     4→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 633
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/633](https://www.erdosproblems.com/633)
    23→
    24→Classify those triangles which can only be cut into a square number of congruent
    25→triangles.
    26→
    27→Any triangle can be cut into $n^2$ congruent triangles for any $n \geq 1$. Soifer [So09]
    28→proved that there exists at least one triangle (e.g. one with sides $\sqrt{2}$, $\sqrt{3}$,
    29→$\sqrt{4}$) which can only be cut into a square number of congruent triangles. In fact,
    30→Soifer proved that any triangle for which the angles and sides are both
    31→integrally independent has this property. The full classification remains open.
    32→
    33→[So09] Soifer, A., *The Mathematical Coloring Book*, Springer, 2009.
    34→-/
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos633
    37→
    38→/-- Squared Euclidean distance between two points in $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    39→noncomputable def sqDist (p q : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    40→  (p.1 - q.1) ^ 2 + (p.2 - q.2) ^ 2
    41→
    42→/-- Multiset of squared side lengths of a triangle.
    43→Two triangles are congruent (by SSS) iff these multisets agree. -/
    44→noncomputable def sideLengthsSq (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Multiset ℝ :=
    45→  ↑[sqDist A B, sqDist B C, sqDist A C]
    46→
    47→/-- A triangle is non-degenerate (vertices not collinear). -/
    48→def NonDegenerate (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    49→  (B.1 - A.1) * (C.2 - A.2) - (C.1 - A.1) * (B.2 - A.2) ≠ 0
    50→
    51→/-- The closed triangular region: convex hull of vertices $A$, $B$, $C$.
    52→A point $p$ lies in the triangle iff $p = \alpha A + \beta B + \gamma C$ for some
    53→$\alpha, \beta, \gamma \geq 0$ with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$. -/
    54→def triangleRegion (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    55→  {p | ∃ (α β γ : ℝ), 0 ≤ α ∧ 0 ≤ β ∧ 0 ≤ γ ∧ α + β + γ = 1 ∧
    56→    p.1 = α * A.1 + β * B.1 + γ * C.1 ∧
    57→    p.2 = α * A.2 + β * B.2 + γ * C.2}
    58→
    59→/-- The open interior of a triangular region: strictly positive
    60→barycentric coordinates. -/
    61→def triangleInterior (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    62→  {p | ∃ (α β γ : ℝ), 0 < α ∧ 0 < β ∧ 0 < γ ∧ α + β + γ = 1 ∧
    63→    p.1 = α * A.1 + β * B.1 + γ * C.1 ∧
    64→    p.2 = α * A.2 + β * B.2 + γ * C.2}
    65→
    66→/-- Triangle $(A, B, C)$ can be dissected into exactly $n$ pairwise-congruent
    67→non-degenerate triangles that tile it: each is contained in the original,
    68→their interiors are pairwise disjoint, and their union covers the original. -/
    69→def CanDissectInto (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    70→  ∃ (V : Fin n → (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ)),
    71→    -- All sub-triangles are non-degenerate
    72→    (∀ i, NonDegenerate (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2) ∧
    73→    -- All sub-triangles are mutually congruent (same squared side lengths)
    74→    (∀ i j, sideLengthsSq (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 =
    75→            sideLengthsSq (V j).1 (V j).2.1 (V j).2.2) ∧
    76→    -- Each sub-triangle is contained in the original
    77→    (∀ i, triangleRegion (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 ⊆
    78→           triangleRegion A B C) ∧
    79→    -- Pairwise disjoint interiors
    80→    (∀ i j, i ≠ j →
    81→      triangleInterior (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 ∩
    82→      triangleInterior (V j).1 (V j).2.1 (V j).2.2 = ∅) ∧
    83→    -- Union covers the original triangle
    84→    (∀ p, p ∈ triangleRegion A B C →
    85→      ∃ i, p ∈ triangleRegion (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2)
    86→
    87→/-- Euclidean distance (side length) between two points. -/
    88→noncomputable def sideLength (p q : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    89→  Real.sqrt (sqDist p q)
    90→
    91→/-- The sides of a triangle are integrally independent: no nontrivial integer
    92→linear combination of the three side lengths equals zero. -/
    93→def IntIndepSides (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    94→  ∀ (a₁ a₂ a₃ : ℤ),
    95→    ↑a₁ * sideLength B C + ↑a₂ * sideLength A C +
    96→      ↑a₃ * sideLength A B = 0 →
    97→    a₁ = 0 ∧ a₂ = 0 ∧ a₃ = 0
    98→
    99→/-- The angle at vertex $P$ in triangle $PQR$, via the dot-product formula:
   100→$\arccos\bigl(\frac{\vec{PQ} \cdot \vec{PR}}{|PQ| \cdot |PR|}\bigr)$.
   101→Returns a value in $[0, \pi]$. -/
   102→noncomputable def angle (P Q R : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
   103→  Real.arccos (((Q.1 - P.1) * (R.1 - P.1) + (Q.2 - P.2) * (R.2 - P.2)) /
   104→    (sideLength P Q * sideLength P R))
   105→
   106→/-- The angles of a triangle are integrally independent: the only integer
   107→linear combination of the angles that equals an integer multiple of $\pi$
   108→is the trivial one (all coefficients equal). This accounts for the
   109→constraint $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \pi$. -/
   110→def IntIndepAngles (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
   111→  ∀ (a₁ a₂ a₃ k : ℤ),
   112→    ↑a₁ * angle A B C + ↑a₂ * angle B A C +
   113→      ↑a₃ * angle C A B = ↑k * Real.pi →
   114→    a₁ = a₂ ∧ a₂ = a₃
   115→
   116→/--
   117→Every non-degenerate triangle can be dissected into $n^2$ congruent triangles
   118→for any positive integer $n$.
   119→-/
   120→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   121→theorem erdos_633.variants.square_dissection
   122→    (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) (hnd : NonDegenerate A B C)
   123→    (n : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) :
   124→    CanDissectInto A B C (n ^ 2) := by
   125→  sorry
   126→
   127→/--
   128→There exists a non-degenerate triangle which can be dissected into $n$
   129→congruent triangles only when $n$ is a perfect square. [So09]
   130→-/
   131→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   132→theorem erdos_633.variants.existence :
   133→    ∃ A B C : ℝ × ℝ, NonDegenerate A B C ∧
   134→    ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n → CanDissectInto A B C n → ∃ m : ℕ, n = m ^ 2 := by
   135→  sorry
   136→
   137→/--
   138→If a non-degenerate triangle has integrally independent sides and angles,
   139→then it can only be dissected into a perfect square number of congruent
   140→triangles. [So09]
   141→-/
   142→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   143→theorem erdos_633.variants.soifer
   144→    (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) (hnd : NonDegenerate A B C)
   145→    (hsides : IntIndepSides A B C) (hangles : IntIndepAngles A B C) :
   146→    ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n → CanDissectInto A B C n → ∃ m : ℕ, n = m ^ 2 := by
   147→  sorry
   148→
   149→/--
   150→Classify those non-degenerate triangles that can only be cut into a perfect
   151→square number of congruent triangles. The full classification remains open.
   152→-/
   153→@[category research open, AMS 51]
   154→theorem erdos_633 :
   155→    {T : (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ) |
   156→      NonDegenerate T.1 T.2.1 T.2.2 ∧
   157→      ∀ n, 1 ≤ n → CanDissectInto T.1 T.2.1 T.2.2 n → ∃ m, n = m ^ 2} =
   158→    answer(sorry) := by sorry
   159→
   160→end Erdos633
   161→

    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 353
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/353](https://www.erdosproblems.com/353)
    23→
    24→Erdős asked whether every measurable subset of $\mathbb{R}^2$ with infinite Lebesgue measure
    25→must contain the vertices of an isosceles trapezoid of area $1$, and similarly for isosceles
    26→triangles, right-angled triangles, and cyclic quadrilaterals of area $1$.
    27→
    28→[Er83d] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on combinatorial geometry_.
    29→
    30→[Ko25] Koizumi, S., _On geometric configurations in measurable sets of infinite measure_.
    31→
    32→[KoPr24] Kovač, V. and Predojević, B., _Cyclic quadrilaterals in measurable sets_.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open MeasureTheory
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos353
    38→
    39→/-- Squared Euclidean distance between two points in $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    40→noncomputable def sqDist (p q : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    41→  (p.1 - q.1) ^ 2 + (p.2 - q.2) ^ 2
    42→
    43→/-- Area of a triangle with vertices $p_1$, $p_2$, $p_3$ via the cross product formula. -/
    44→noncomputable def triangleArea (p₁ p₂ p₃ : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    45→  |(p₂.1 - p₁.1) * (p₃.2 - p₁.2) - (p₃.1 - p₁.1) * (p₂.2 - p₁.2)| / 2
    46→
    47→/-- Area of a quadrilateral with consecutive vertices $p_1$, $p_2$, $p_3$, $p_4$ via the
    48→shoelace formula. -/
    49→noncomputable def quadArea (p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    50→  |(p₁.1 * p₂.2 - p₂.1 * p₁.2 + p₂.1 * p₃.2 - p₃.1 * p₂.2 +
    51→    p₃.1 * p₄.2 - p₄.1 * p₃.2 + p₄.1 * p₁.2 - p₁.1 * p₄.2)| / 2
    52→
    53→/-- Two vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are parallel (cross product is zero). -/
    54→def Parallel (v w : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    55→  v.1 * w.2 = v.2 * w.1
    56→
    57→/-- Three points form an isosceles triangle: all distinct with at least two sides
    58→of equal length. -/
    59→def IsIsoscelesTriangle (p₁ p₂ p₃ : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    60→  p₁ ≠ p₂ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₃ ∧
    61→  (sqDist p₁ p₂ = sqDist p₁ p₃ ∨
    62→   sqDist p₁ p₂ = sqDist p₂ p₃ ∨
    63→   sqDist p₁ p₃ = sqDist p₂ p₃)
    64→
    65→/-- Three points form a right-angled triangle: all distinct with one angle equal
    66→to $90°$ (dot product of adjacent edges is zero at some vertex). -/
    67→def IsRightTriangle (p₁ p₂ p₃ : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    68→  p₁ ≠ p₂ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₃ ∧
    69→  ((p₂.1 - p₁.1) * (p₃.1 - p₁.1) + (p₂.2 - p₁.2) * (p₃.2 - p₁.2) = 0 ∨
    70→   (p₁.1 - p₂.1) * (p₃.1 - p₂.1) + (p₁.2 - p₂.2) * (p₃.2 - p₂.2) = 0 ∨
    71→   (p₁.1 - p₃.1) * (p₂.1 - p₃.1) + (p₁.2 - p₃.2) * (p₂.2 - p₃.2) = 0)
    72→
    73→/-- Four points (in order) form an isosceles trapezoid: exactly one pair of
    74→parallel opposite sides, with the non-parallel sides (legs) of equal length.
    75→Here $p_1 p_2 \parallel p_4 p_3$ are the parallel sides and $p_1 p_4$, $p_2 p_3$
    76→are the equal legs. -/
    77→def IsIsoscelesTrapezoid (p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    78→  p₁ ≠ p₂ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₄ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₄ ∧ p₃ ≠ p₄ ∧
    79→  Parallel (p₂.1 - p₁.1, p₂.2 - p₁.2) (p₃.1 - p₄.1, p₃.2 - p₄.2) ∧
    80→  sqDist p₁ p₄ = sqDist p₂ p₃ ∧
    81→  ¬ Parallel (p₄.1 - p₁.1, p₄.2 - p₁.2) (p₃.1 - p₂.1, p₃.2 - p₂.2)
    82→
    83→/-- Four distinct points lie on a common circle. -/
    84→def IsCyclicQuadrilateral (p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    85→  p₁ ≠ p₂ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₄ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₄ ∧ p₃ ≠ p₄ ∧
    86→  ∃ (c : ℝ × ℝ) (r_sq : ℝ), 0 < r_sq ∧
    87→    sqDist c p₁ = r_sq ∧ sqDist c p₂ = r_sq ∧
    88→    sqDist c p₃ = r_sq ∧ sqDist c p₄ = r_sq
    89→
    90→/--
    91→Erdős Problem 353, Part 1 [Er83d]:
    92→
    93→Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ be a measurable set with infinite Lebesgue measure. Must $A$
    94→contain the vertices of an isosceles trapezoid of area $1$?
    95→
    96→Proved by Koizumi [Ko25].
    97→-/
    98→@[category research solved, AMS 28 52]
    99→theorem erdos_353 : answer(True) ↔
   100→    ∀ (A : Set (ℝ × ℝ)), MeasurableSet A → volume A = ⊤ →
   101→    ∃ p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ : ℝ × ℝ,
   102→      p₁ ∈ A ∧ p₂ ∈ A ∧ p₃ ∈ A ∧ p₄ ∈ A ∧
   103→      IsIsoscelesTrapezoid p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ ∧
   104→      quadArea p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ = 1 := by
   105→  sorry
   106→
   107→/--
   108→Erdős Problem 353, Part 2 [Er83d]:
   109→
   110→Must a measurable set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ with infinite measure contain the vertices of
   111→an isosceles triangle of area $1$?
   112→
   113→Proved by Koizumi [Ko25].
   114→-/
   115→@[category research solved, AMS 28 52]
   116→theorem erdos_353.variants.isosceles_triangle : answer(True) ↔
   117→    ∀ (A : Set (ℝ × ℝ)), MeasurableSet A → volume A = ⊤ →
   118→    ∃ p₁ p₂ p₃ : ℝ × ℝ,
   119→      p₁ ∈ A ∧ p₂ ∈ A ∧ p₃ ∈ A ∧
   120→      IsIsoscelesTriangle p₁ p₂ p₃ ∧
   121→      triangleArea p₁ p₂ p₃ = 1 := by
   122→  sorry
   123→
   124→/--
   125→Erdős Problem 353, Part 3 [Er83d]:
   126→
   127→Must a measurable set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ with infinite measure contain the vertices of
   128→a right-angled triangle of area $1$?
   129→
   130→Proved by Koizumi [Ko25].
   131→-/
   132→@[category research solved, AMS 28 52]
   133→theorem erdos_353.variants.right_triangle : answer(True) ↔
   134→    ∀ (A : Set (ℝ × ℝ)), MeasurableSet A → volume A = ⊤ →
   135→    ∃ p₁ p₂ p₃ : ℝ × ℝ,
   136→      p₁ ∈ A ∧ p₂ ∈ A ∧ p₃ ∈ A ∧
   137→      IsRightTriangle p₁ p₂ p₃ ∧
   138→      triangleArea p₁ p₂ p₃ = 1 := by
   139→  sorry
   140→
   141→/--
   142→Erdős Problem 353, Part 4 [Er83d]:
   143→
   144→Must a measurable set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ with infinite measure contain four distinct
   145→points on a common circle forming a quadrilateral of area $1$?
   146→
   147→Proved by Kovač and Predojević [KoPr24].
   148→-/
   149→@[category research solved, AMS 28 52]
   150→theorem erdos_353.variants.cyclic_quadrilateral : answer(True) ↔
   151→    ∀ (A : Set (ℝ × ℝ)), MeasurableSet A → volume A = ⊤ →
   152→    ∃ p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ : ℝ × ℝ,
   153→      p₁ ∈ A ∧ p₂ ∈ A ∧ p₃ ∈ A ∧ p₄ ∈ A ∧
   154→      IsCyclicQuadrilateral p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ ∧
   155→      quadArea p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ = 1 := by
   156→  sorry
   157→
   158→end Erdos353
   159→

    22→def Triplewise {α : Type*} (r : α → α → α → Prop) : Prop :=
    23→  ∀ ⦃i j k ⦄, i ≠ j → j ≠ k → i ≠ k → r i j k
    24→
    25→namespace Set
    26→
    27→variable {α : Type*} {r : α → α → α → Prop} {s t : Set α} {x y z : α}
    28→
    29→/--
    30→The ternary relation `r` holds triplewise on the set `s`
    31→if `r x y z` for all *distinct* `x y z ∈ s`.
    32→-/
    33→protected def Triplewise (s : Set α) (r : α → α → α → Prop) : Prop :=
    34→  ∀ ⦃x⦄, x ∈ s → ∀ ⦃y⦄, y ∈ s → ∀ ⦃z⦄, z ∈ s → x ≠ y → y ≠ z → x ≠ z → r x y z
    35→
    36→protected theorem Triplewise.eq (hs : s.Triplewise r)
    37→    (hx : x ∈ s) (hy : y ∈ s) (hz : z ∈ s) (h : ¬r x y z) :
    38→    x = y ∨ y = z ∨ x = z := by
    39→  have := (h <| hs hx hy hz · · ·)
    40→  tauto
    41→
    42→theorem Triplewise.mono (h : t ⊆ s) (hs : s.Triplewise r) :
    43→    t.Triplewise r :=
    44→  fun _ hx _ hy _ hz hxy hyz hxz ↦ hs (h hx) (h hy) (h hz) hxy hyz hxz
    45→
    46→@[simp]
    47→theorem Triplewise_univ_iff :
    48→    Set.univ.Triplewise r ↔ Triplewise r := by
    49→  simp [Set.Triplewise, Triplewise]
    50→
    51→theorem triplewise_of_encard_lt (r : α → α → α → Prop)
    52→    (h : s.encard < 3) : s.Triplewise r := by
    53→  unfold Set.Triplewise
    54→  contrapose! h
    55→  obtain ⟨x, hx, y, hy, z, hz, hxy, hyz, hxz, _⟩ := h
    56→  trans encard {x, y, z}
    57→  · simp [Set.encard_insert_of_notMem, *]
    58→    norm_num
    59→  · exact encard_le_encard_of_injOn (by simp [MapsTo, hx, hy, hz]) (injOn_id _)
    60→
    61→@[simp]
    62→theorem triplewise_empty (r : α → α → α → Prop) :
    63→    Set.Triplewise ∅ r :=
    64→  (notMem_empty · · |>.elim)
    65→
    66→@[simp]
    67→theorem triplewise_singleton (r : α → α → α → Prop) :
    68→    Set.Triplewise {x} r := by
    69→  apply triplewise_of_encard_lt
    70→  simp
    71→
    72→@[simp]
    73→theorem triplewise_pair
    74→    (r : α → α → α → Prop) : Set.Triplewise {x, y} r := by
    75→  apply triplewise_of_encard_lt
    76→  apply (encard_insert_le {y} x).trans_lt
    77→  simp
    78→  norm_num
    79→
    80→theorem triplewise_insert :
    81→    (insert x s).Triplewise r ↔ s.Triplewise r ∧
    82→    ∀ y ∈ s, ∀ z ∈ s, x ≠ y → x ≠ z → y ≠ z → r x y z ∧ r y x z ∧ r y z x := by
    83→  refine ⟨fun hxs ↦ ?_, fun ⟨hs, hxs⟩ ↦ ?_⟩
    84→  · refine ⟨hxs.mono (subset_insert x s), fun y hy z hz hxy hxz hyz ↦ ⟨?_, ?_, ?_⟩⟩
    85→    · exact hxs (mem_insert x s) (mem_insert_of_mem x hy) (mem_insert_of_mem x hz) hxy hyz hxz
    86→    · exact hxs (mem_insert_of_mem x hy) (mem_insert x s) (mem_insert_of_mem x hz) hxy.symm hxz hyz
    87→    · exact hxs (mem_insert_of_mem x hy) (mem_insert_of_mem x hz)
    88→        (mem_insert x s) hyz hxz.symm hxy.symm
    89→  · intro w hw y hy z hz hwy hyz hwz
    90→    rw [mem_insert_iff] at hw hy hz
    91→    obtain rfl | hw := hw
    92→    · refine (hxs y ?_ z ?_ hwy hwz hyz).1
    93→      · simpa [hwy.symm] using hy
    94→      · simpa [hwz.symm] using hz
    95→    obtain rfl | hy := hy
    96→    · exact (hxs w hw z (by simpa [hyz.symm] using hz) hwy.symm hyz hwz).2.1
    97→    obtain rfl | hz := hz
    98→    · exact (hxs w hw y hy hwz.symm hyz.symm hwy).2.2
    99→    exact hs hw hy hz hwy hyz hwz
   100→
   101→theorem triplewise_insert_of_not_mem (hx : x ∉ s) :
   102→    (insert x s).Triplewise r ↔ s.Triplewise r ∧
   103→    ∀ y ∈ s, ∀ z ∈ s, y ≠ z → r x y z ∧ r y x z ∧ r y z x := by
   104→  refine Set.triplewise_insert.trans <| and_congr_right' <| forall₄_congr ?_
   105→  intro y hy z hz
   106→  simp [(ne_of_mem_of_not_mem hy hx).symm, (ne_of_mem_of_not_mem hz hx).symm]
   107→
   108→protected theorem Triplewise.insert (hs : s.Triplewise r)
   109→    (h : ∀ y ∈ s, ∀ z ∈ s, x ≠ y → x ≠ z → y ≠ z → r x y z ∧ r y x z ∧ r y z x) :
   110→    (insert x s).Triplewise r :=
   111→  Set.triplewise_insert.mpr ⟨hs, h⟩
   112→
   113→protected theorem Triplewise.insert_of_not_mem
   114→    (hx : x ∉ s) (hs : s.Triplewise r)
   115→    (h : ∀ y ∈ s, ∀ z ∈ s, y ≠ z → r x y z ∧ r y x z ∧ r y z x) :
   116→    (insert x s).Triplewise r :=
   117→  (Set.triplewise_insert_of_not_mem hx).mpr ⟨hs, h⟩

   119→theorem triplewise_set_pred_iff {S : Set α} {P : Set α → Prop} :
   120→    S.Triplewise (P {·, ·, ·}) ↔ ∀ T ⊆ S, T.ncard = 3 → P T := by
   121→  constructor
   122→  · intro h T hT T_ncard
   123→    obtain ⟨x, y, z, hxy, hxz, hyz, rfl⟩ := ncard_eq_three.mp T_ncard
   124→    simp_rw [insert_subset_iff, singleton_subset_iff] at hT
   125→    exact h hT.1 hT.2.1 hT.2.2 hxy hyz hxz
   126→  · intro h x hx y hy z hz hxy hyz hxz
   127→    refine h _ ?_ (ncard_eq_three.mpr ⟨x, y, z, hxy, hxz, hyz, rfl⟩)
   128→    simp [insert_subset_iff, hx, hy, hz]
   129→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 223
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/223](https://www.erdosproblems.com/223)
    23→
    24→Let $d \geq 2$ and $n \geq 2$. Let $f_d(n)$ be maximal such that there exists
    25→some set of $n$ points $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$, with diameter $1$, in which
    26→the distance $1$ occurs between $f_d(n)$ many pairs of points in $A$.
    27→Estimate $f_d(n)$.
    28→
    29→Originally a conjecture of Vázsonyi [Er46b].
    30→
    31→Known results:
    32→- Hopf and Pannwitz [HoPa34] proved $f_2(n) = n$.
    33→- Grünbaum [Gr56], Heppes [He56], and Strasziewicz [St57] showed $f_3(n) = 2n - 2$.
    34→- Erdős [Er60b] proved that for $d \geq 4$,
    35→  $f_d(n) = \left(\frac{p-1}{2p} + o(1)\right) n^2$ where $p = \lfloor d/2 \rfloor$.
    36→- Swanepoel [Sw09] gave exact values for all $d \geq 4$ and sufficiently large $n$.
    37→-/
    38→
    39→open Classical
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos223
    42→
    43→/-- The number of unordered pairs $\{x, y\}$ in $A$ with $x \neq y$ and
    44→$\operatorname{dist}(x, y) = 1$. Defined as half the ordered pair count
    45→(always even by symmetry of $\operatorname{dist}$). -/
    46→noncomputable def unitDistPairs {d : ℕ} (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d))) : ℕ :=
    47→  ((A ×ˢ A).filter (fun p => p.1 ≠ p.2 ∧ dist p.1 p.2 = 1)).card / 2
    48→
    49→/--
    50→Erdős Problem 223 ($d \geq 4$), upper bound — Erdős [Er60b]:
    51→For $d \geq 4$, let $p = \lfloor d/2 \rfloor$. For every $\varepsilon > 0$, for sufficiently
    52→large $n$, any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^d$ of diameter $\leq 1$ have at most
    53→$\left(\frac{p-1}{2p} + \varepsilon\right) n^2$ unit-distance pairs.
    54→-/
    55→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    56→theorem erdos_223 (d : ℕ) (hd : 4 ≤ d) (ε : ℝ) (hε : 0 < ε) :
    57→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d))),
    58→      N₀ ≤ A.card →
    59→      (∀ x ∈ A, ∀ y ∈ A, dist x y ≤ 1) →
    60→      (unitDistPairs A : ℝ) ≤
    61→        ((↑(d / 2) - 1) / (2 * ↑(d / 2)) + ε) * (A.card : ℝ) ^ 2 := by
    62→  sorry
    63→
    64→/--
    65→Erdős Problem 223 ($d \geq 4$), lower bound — Erdős [Er60b]:
    66→For $d \geq 4$, let $p = \lfloor d/2 \rfloor$. For every $\varepsilon > 0$, for sufficiently
    67→large $n$, there exist $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^d$ of diameter $\leq 1$ with at least
    68→$\left(\frac{p-1}{2p} - \varepsilon\right) n^2$ unit-distance pairs.
    69→-/
    70→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    71→theorem erdos_223.variants.d_ge4_lower (d : ℕ) (hd : 4 ≤ d) (ε : ℝ) (hε : 0 < ε) :
    72→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N₀ ≤ n →
    73→      ∃ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d)),
    74→        A.card = n ∧
    75→        (∀ x ∈ A, ∀ y ∈ A, dist x y ≤ 1) ∧
    76→        ((↑(d / 2) - 1) / (2 * ↑(d / 2)) - ε) * (n : ℝ) ^ 2 ≤ (unitDistPairs A : ℝ) := by
    77→  sorry
    78→
    79→/--
    80→Erdős Problem 223 ($d = 2$), Hopf–Pannwitz [HoPa34]:
    81→Among $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with all pairwise distances $\leq 1$, the distance $1$
    82→occurs between at most $n$ pairs.
    83→-/
    84→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    85→theorem erdos_223.variants.d2_upper (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)))
    86→    (hdiam : ∀ x ∈ A, ∀ y ∈ A, dist x y ≤ 1) :
    87→    unitDistPairs A ≤ A.card := by
    88→  sorry
    89→
    90→/--
    91→Erdős Problem 223 ($d = 2$), tightness:
    92→For every $n \geq 2$, there exist $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with diameter $1$ and
    93→exactly $n$ pairs at distance $1$.
    94→-/
    95→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    96→theorem erdos_223.variants.d2_tight (n : ℕ) (hn : 2 ≤ n) :
    97→    ∃ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    98→      A.card = n ∧
    99→      (∀ x ∈ A, ∀ y ∈ A, dist x y ≤ 1) ∧
   100→      unitDistPairs A = n := by
   101→  sorry
   102→
   103→/--
   104→Erdős Problem 223 ($d = 3$), Grünbaum–Heppes–Strasziewicz [Gr56, He56, St57]:
   105→Among $n \geq 2$ points in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with all pairwise distances $\leq 1$, the distance $1$
   106→occurs between at most $2n - 2$ pairs.
   107→-/
   108→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   109→theorem erdos_223.variants.d3_upper (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3)))
   110→    (hdiam : ∀ x ∈ A, ∀ y ∈ A, dist x y ≤ 1)
   111→    (hcard : 2 ≤ A.card) :
   112→    unitDistPairs A ≤ 2 * A.card - 2 := by
   113→  sorry
   114→
   115→/--
   116→Erdős Problem 223 ($d = 3$), tightness:
   117→For every $n \geq 2$, there exist $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with diameter $1$ and
   118→exactly $2n - 2$ pairs at distance $1$.
   119→-/
   120→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
   121→theorem erdos_223.variants.d3_tight (n : ℕ) (hn : 2 ≤ n) :
   122→    ∃ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3)),
   123→      A.card = n ∧
   124→      (∀ x ∈ A, ∀ y ∈ A, dist x y ≤ 1) ∧
   125→      unitDistPairs A = 2 * n - 2 := by
   126→  sorry
   127→
   128→end Erdos223
   129→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 98
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/98](https://www.erdosproblems.com/98)
    23→
    24→Let $h(n)$ be the minimum number of distinct distances determined by $n$ points in
    25→$\mathbb{R}^2$ with no three collinear and no four concyclic. Does $h(n)/n \to \infty$?
    26→-/
    27→
    28→namespace Erdos98
    29→
    30→/--
    31→A finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has no three collinear if every three-element subset
    32→is not collinear (i.e., no line contains three or more of the points).
    33→-/
    34→def NoThreeCollinear (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    35→  ∀ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    36→    S ⊆ P → S.card = 3 → ¬Collinear ℝ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)))
    37→
    38→/--
    39→Four points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are concyclic if they all lie on a common circle, i.e.,
    40→there exists a center and positive radius such that all four points are
    41→equidistant from the center.
    42→-/
    43→def FourPointsConcyclic (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    44→  ∃ c : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2), ∃ r : ℝ, r > 0 ∧ ∀ p ∈ S, dist p c = r
    45→
    46→/--
    47→A finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has no four concyclic if every four-element subset
    48→does not lie on a common circle.
    49→-/
    50→def NoFourConcyclic (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    51→  ∀ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    52→    S ⊆ P → S.card = 4 → ¬FourPointsConcyclic S
    53→
    54→/--
    55→The number of distinct pairwise distances determined by a finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
    56→-/
    57→noncomputable def distinctDistanceCount (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    58→  Set.ncard {d : ℝ | ∃ p ∈ P, ∃ q ∈ P, p ≠ q ∧ dist p q = d}
    59→
    60→/--
    61→Erdős Problem 98:
    62→Let $h(n)$ be the minimum number of distinct distances determined by any $n$ points
    63→in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with no three collinear and no four concyclic. Does $h(n)/n \to \infty$?
    64→
    65→Formally: for every $C > 0$ there exists $N$ such that for all $n \geq N$ and every
    66→set $P$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with no three collinear and no four concyclic,
    67→the number of distinct distances is at least $C \cdot n$.
    68→-/
    69→@[category research open, AMS 5 52]
    70→theorem erdos_98 : answer(sorry) ↔
    71→    ∀ C : ℝ, C > 0 →
    72→      ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N →
    73→        ∀ P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    74→          P.card = n →
    75→          NoThreeCollinear P →
    76→          NoFourConcyclic P →
    77→          (distinctDistanceCount P : ℝ) ≥ C * (n : ℝ) := by
    78→  sorry
    79→
    80→end Erdos98
